retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- NRTIs
retrieved_docs.content: NRTIs compete with natural deoxynucleotides for incorporation into a growing viral DNA chain. However, NRTIs lack a 3'-hydroxyl group on the deoxyribose moiety. This difference results in incorporating an NRTI, and the next incoming deoxynucleotide cannot form the following 5', 3' phosphodiester bond needed to extend the DNA chain. The result is a chain termination in DNA synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Atazanavir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The viral integrase enzyme is essential for integrating proviral DNA into the host chromosome. FDA-approved viral integrase inhibitors include bictegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. [17] Owing to the essential role of HIV protease in producing the mature virus, HIV protease has been the target of anti-HIV drugs. FDA-approved protease inhibitors include amprenavir, atazanavir, darunavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, lopinavir, nelfinavir, saquinavir, and tipranavir. These drugs are peptide mimetics that compete with the Gag and Gag-Pol proteins, which are natural substrates of HIV protease. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- NNRTIs
retrieved_docs.content: NNRTIs block reverse transcriptase (RT) by directly binding to the enzyme. Though NNRTIs do not get incorporated into the viral DNA, they inhibit the movement of protein domains of RT that are essential to carrying out the DNA synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Integrase Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase inhibitors block the action of integrase, preventing the viral genome from inserting itself into the DNA of a host cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Atazanavir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The first drug approved as an anti-HIV agent was azidothymidine (zidovudine)—a nucleoside analog of thymidine without a 3’-hydroxyl group on the sugar. [1] Cellular triphosphorylation of the 5’-hydroxyl group of azidothymidine leads to incorporating this nucleotide by viral reverse transcriptase during reverse transcription. However, the elongation of the DNA chain is prematurely terminated because of the absence of the 3’-hydroxyl group. Additional nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors comprise dideoxycytidine, dideoxyinosine, idoxuridine, ribavirin, stavudine, and trifluridine. Lamivudine features a modified sugar, whereas idoxuridine, ribavirin, and trifluridine have modified bases.

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: NRTIs require intracellular phosphorylation via host enzymes before they can inhibit viral replication. These agents are nucleoside or nucleotide analogs with an absent hydroxyl at the 3’ end that are incorporated into the growing viral DNA strand. They competitively bind to reverse transcriptase and cause premature DNA chain termination as they inhibit 3’ to 5’ phosphodiester bond formation. Examples include: abacavir, didanosine, lamivudine, stavudine, tenofovir, and zidovudine

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Postattachment Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: This class is a monoclonal antibody that binds CD4, inhibiting viral entry into the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Plasma protein binding (PPB) for nirmatrelvir is 69% (coadministered with ritonavir), PPB of ritonavir 98-99%. The mean volume of Distribution for nirmatrelvir is 104.7 L (coadministered with ritonavir).

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Mechanism of Action -- Non-nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (NNRTIs)
retrieved_docs.content: NNRTIs bind to HIV reverse transcriptase at an allosteric, hydrophobic site. These agents cause a stereochemical change within reverse transcriptase, thus inhibiting nucleoside binding and inhibition of DNA polymerase. Examples include delavirdine, efavirenz, nevirapine, and rilpivirine. Protease inhibitors (PIs) [25] PIs competitively inhibit the proteolytic cleavage of the gag/pol polyproteins in HIV-infected cells. These agents result in immature, non-infectious virions. PIs are generally used in patients who fail their initial HAART regimen and should be administered with boosting agents such as ritonavir or cobicistat. Examples include atazanavir, darunavir, indinavir. Integrase Strand Transfer Inhibitors (INSTIs) [26] [27] Integrase inhibitors bind viral integrase and prevent viral DNA from being incorporated into the host cell chromosome. Examples include: dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: The time to maximum plasma concentration (Tmax) for nirmatrelvir(coadministered with ritonavir) is approximately 3 hours. Administering high-fat meals with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir increases the rate and extent of absorption.

retrieved_docs.title: Atazanavir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibition of the protease cleavage of these precursor proteins results in incomplete packaging of the virus. Protease inhibitors also have various off-target interactions, such as atazanavir's inhibition of the UGT1A1 enzyme responsible for bilirubin glucuronidation. This inhibition can result in the accumulation of bilirubin in the serum. [19] However, atazanavir use is associated with fewer cardiac and lipid profile adverse effects than other protease inhibitors. [20] Furthermore, enfuvirtide and maraviroc are known as viral entry inhibitors. Enfuvirtide acts as a peptide mimic to a crucial region of the HIV-1 glycoprotein-41 necessary for HIV-1 entry into host cells. Conversely, maraviroc functions as a chemokine receptor CCR5 inhibitor, targeting the co-receptor that HIV utilizes for host cell entry. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Ritonavir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ritonavir works by binding to HIV-1 protease. HIV-1 protease causes cleavage of protein precursors generating new viral particles. Protease inhibitors disrupt this cleavage process, interrupting the production of new viral particles. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetic Enhancers
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacokinetic enhancers inhibit human CYP3A protein, increasing plasma concentration of other anti-HIV drugs. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Darunavir: Coadministration of formulations is contraindicated with medications highly dependent on CYP3A for clearance and for which elevated plasma concentrations are associated with serious and life-threatening events.

retrieved_docs.title: Ritonavir -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In combination with other standard ARV medications, lopinavir/ritonavir has provided significant virological suppression and better immune outcomes in both treatment-naive and treatment-experienced patients. [2] Because of its mechanism of action, ritonavir is currently under investigation for its use in treating some forms of cancer. [3] In 2014, the FDA approved a combination drug of ombitasvir/paritaprevir/ritonavir and dasabuvir to treat hepatitis C virus (HCV) genotype 1. [4] It is highly efficacious with minimal side effects. Additionally, lopinavir/ritonavir was FDA approved in early 2000.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Protease Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors bind HIV-1 protease and block proteolytic cleavage of protein precursors necessary for producing viral particles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, comprise a group of medications that target the HIV protease enzyme to prevent viral replication. Key members of this protease inhibitor class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each sharing the common mechanism of protease inhibition while differing in pharmacokinetic properties and clinical use. Recognizing these agents as part of the broader protease inhibitor, direct‑acting antiviral category is essential for selecting appropriate antiretroviral regimens and optimizing patient care in HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals constitute a broad pharmacologic group that is organized by therapeutic indication, mechanism of action, and anatomical site of use. Within the hepatitis C subclass, ribavirin and the oligopeptide‑derived telaprevir illustrate the use of peptide scaffolds in antiviral design. The HIV‑combination subclass includes fixed‑dose regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, both belonging to the larger HIV‑combination antiviral group. Additional antiretrovirals for HIV infection comprise the entry inhibitor enfuvirtide, the fusion‑inhibitor ibalizumab, nucleoside and nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) such as abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, lamivudine, emtricitabine, stavudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine, non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) including delavirdine (and delavirdine‑mesylate), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine (the latter contraindicated with pantoprazole), and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are a class of antiretroviral agents that act by binding to HIV reverse transcriptase and inhibiting viral replication. The drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin, and rilpivirine are all members of the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, anticipate class‑related effects, and make informed choices when selecting or switching antiretroviral therapy for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs constitute a group of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct non‑nucleoside site, thereby preventing viral RNA transcription and replication. By sharing this common mechanism of action, delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are used in combination regimens to treat HIV infection, offering an alternative to nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and contributing to the overall effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals form a broad pharmacologic class organized by therapeutic target, chemical structure and mechanism of action and include agents for hepatitis C virus (ribavirin, telaprevir), HIV combination therapies (fixed‑dose combinations such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine), cyclic‑amine antivirals (rimantadine), neuraminidase inhibitors (zanamivir), non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) (delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, nevirapine, rilpivirine, etravirine), nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) (abacavir, didanosine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, tenofovir alafenamide, tenofovir, emtricitabine/tenofovir, abacavir/lamivudine), integrase inhibitors (raltegravir, cabotegravir, dolutegravir), CCR5 antagonist (maraviroc), entry inhibitors (enfuvirtide, ibalizumab, fostemsavir, bulevirtide), fusion inhibitor (enfuvirtide), other antivirals

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, amprenavir, and tipranavir are all classified as protease inhibitors that belong to the group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug specifically targets viral protease enzymes, a critical step in the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV, thereby providing a mechanism of action that directly interferes with viral maturation. Their shared categorization as direct‑acting antivirals highlights that they act on viral components rather than host cellular processes, which informs clinicians about common therapeutic objectives, potential for cross‑resistance, and similar considerations regarding drug‑drug interactions and adverse effect profiles in antiretroviral treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: subgroup contains cidofovir, while the broader nucleoside/nucleotide group includes brincidofovir and brivudine, the latter mapped to a bromodeoxyuridin scaffold and acting by mechanisms other than reverse‑transcriptase inhibition. HIV‑specific DAAs comprise fixed‑dose combinations such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine, both mapped to a dideoxyadenosin scaffold and functioning as reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. A wide range of antiretroviral agents are used for HIV prevention (e.g., delavirdin‑mesilát, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabin, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudin, nevirapin, raltegravir, rilpivirin, stavudin, zidovudin, enfuvirtide, abacavir, delavirdine) and for HIV treatment (cabotegravir, fostemsavir, maraviroc). The NRTI class includes abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, all of which terminate viral DNA synthesis. Integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir,
